content.problemStatement:
An array BBB of length MMM is called magical if its bitwise AND equals its bitwise XOR.

That is, (B1 & B2 & … & BM)=(B1⊕B2⊕…⊕BM)(B_1 \ \&\ B_2\ \&\ \ldots\ \&\ B_M) = (B_1\oplus B_2\oplus\ldots\oplus B_M)(B1​ & B2​ & … & BM​)=(B1​⊕B2​⊕…⊕BM​) should hold, where &\&& denotes bitwise AND, and ⊕\oplus⊕ denotes bitwise XOR.

For example, [1,1,1][1, 1, 1][1,1,1] is magical because its bitwise AND and bitwise XOR both equal 111, but [1,1][1, 1][1,1] is not magical because its bitwise AND equals 111 while its bitwise XOR equals 000.

You are given an array AAA of length NNN. Count the number of its subarrays that are magical.

More formally, count the number of pairs (L,R)(L, R)(L,R) such that 1≤L≤R≤N1\leq L\leq R\leq N1≤L≤R≤N and the array [AL,AL+1,…,AR][A_L, A_{L+1}, \ldots, A_R][AL​,AL+1​,…,AR​] is magical.


Input Format

The first line of input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of testcases.
Each testcase consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each testcase contains a single integer NNN, denoting the length of the array AAA.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​, denoting the elements of AAA.




Output Format
For each testcase, output on a new line a single integer: the number of magical subarrays of AAA.
constraints: The sum of values of NNN over all the testcases does not exceed 10510^5105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: There are three subarrays of the array [1,1][1, 1][1,1].

L=R=1L = R = 1L=R=1 gives [1][1][1], with bitwise AND and bitwise XOR both equal to 111.
L=1,R=2L = 1, R = 2L=1,R=2 gives [1,1][1, 1][1,1], with AND 111 but XOR 000.
L=R=2L = R = 2L=R=2 gives [1][1][1], with AND and XOR both equal to 111.

So, two subarrays are magical.
Test case 222: The four magical subarrays are [A1],[A2],[A3],[A1,A2,A3][A_1], [A_2], [A_3], [A_1, A_2, A_3][A1​],[A2​],[A3​],[A1​,A2​,A3​].
